list.name: worker_host_app
list.label: Worker app (derived)
list.description: Not a dropdown — the worker Fly app names belonging to $country, resolved from the country-labelled kinowo_worker_throttled. Exists solely so the memory panels' fly_instance_* (host) series can be narrowed to the selected country: those live on the fly-prometheus datasource, which carries no country label, so no on(app) join can reach them. Derived, so re-splitting countries across Fly apps needs no dashboard edit.

list.name: web_host_app
list.label: Web app (derived)
list.description: The web mirror of $worker_host_app, resolved from kinowo_web_movies_served. Same reason: it narrows the host-free series on the web memory panel to the selected country.

panels.title: CPU load by app — %core (busy = work it does; steal = host throttling). Watch step-ups against Deploys.
panels.description: Every app is shared-cpu-1x, so 100 = one full core. '<app> busy' is the work it does (user+system+io); '<app> steal' is CPU the host withheld because demand crossed the shared-CPU credit — near-zero historically, it only appears when busy saturates the core. A step-up that lines up with a purple Deploy marker means that release added load. FLEET-WIDE: shows every country's instances at once and is NOT filtered by the Country dropdown — fly_instance_* carries no country label, so scoping is by app name. The matcher app=~"kinowo.*|showtimes-.*" follows the naming convention (PL web = kinowo, other web = showtimes-<cc>, workers = kinowo-worker[-<cc>]), so a new country appears here the moment it deploys, with no dashboard edit. filmowo has its own dashboard, schowek is separate.

panels.title: CPU credit balance (serving apps — alarm < 500)
panels.description: Shared-cpu-1x credit left per app. Every country's instances, NOT filtered by the Country dropdown (fly_instance_* has no country label). Drives the 'CPU credit exhausted' alert.

panels.title: CPU throttled (rate, fraction of wall-clock)
panels.description: Fraction of wall-clock the host held the app off-CPU once credit ran out. Every country's instances, NOT filtered by the Country dropdown (fly_instance_* has no country label). Drives the 'CPU throttled' and 'Worker CPU starved' alerts.

panels.title: CPU steal %
panels.description: Steal as a share of that app's total CPU time — the same signal as the dashed lines on the top panel, normalised. Every country's instances, NOT filtered by the Country dropdown (fly_instance_* has no country label). Drives the 'CPU steal high' alert.

panels.title: Memory available % (alarm < 8%)
panels.description: Host memory still available, as a fraction of total — the pre-OOM warning. Every country's instances, NOT filtered by the Country dropdown (fly_instance_* has no country label). Drives the 'Memory pressure (pre-OOM)' alert. For the worker's JVM-side breakdown (heap vs non-heap vs -Xmx) see the 'Worker — JVM heap & host free memory' panel below.

panels.title: HTTP latency — web (s)
panels.description: Fly edge-measured response time for the WEB apps only (app=~"kinowo|showtimes-.*" — no workers, they serve no HTTP). One line per percentile PER APP, so every country's web instance is plotted: kinowo = PL, showtimes-<cc> = the rest. NOT filtered by the Country dropdown (fly_app_* has no country label).

panels.title: Worker — throttled (1 = reapers backing off, 0 = full enqueue)
Instance up
Residential proxy — fetches served vs failed (recent 30 min, from web /metrics)
panels.description: The Decodo residential-proxy leg of the cinema scrape, surfaced from the in-app /uptime health (Mongo) via web's /metrics scrape. A flip from all-green 'served by proxy' to all-red 'failed → Zyte fallback' is a silent cost regression — scraping keeps working via Zyte, so no cinema bar goes red. Drives the 'Residential proxy failing' alert.

panels.title: Worker — task flow (per min): enqueued → started → fully worked
panels.description: The deferred-work pipeline on kinowo-worker, from worker /metrics. enqueued = new tasks added to the queue (dedup collapses excluded); started = tasks claimed by a worker; fully worked (done) = handlers that did real work (excludes 'skipped' freshness no-ops). In steady state the three track; started ≫ done means lots of skipped/rescheduled, done ≪ enqueued means a growing backlog.

panels.title: Worker — enqueued (non-duplicate) by type (per min)
panels.description: Rate of tasks ADDED to the queue per task type (kinowo_worker_tasks_enqueued_total{result="added"} — re-enqueues that the dedupKey collapses are excluded, so this is net new work, not churn). The per-type split of the 'enqueued' line in the overall flow panel above. Read with 'started by type' and 'fully-worked throughput by type' as one type's enqueue → claim → done funnel.

panels.title: Worker — started (claimed) by type (per min)
panels.description: Rate of tasks CLAIMED by a worker per task type (kinowo_worker_tasks_started_total). Counts every claim, including ones that then skip (data already fresh) or reschedule, so started ≫ the same type's 'fully-worked throughput' means that type is handing out a lot of no-op work. The per-type split of the 'started' line in the overall flow panel above.

panels.title: Worker — fully-worked throughput by type (per min)
panels.description: rate of done (not skipped) tasks per task type — which kinds of work the worker is actually completing: cinema scrapes, detail enrichment, TMDB/IMDb resolution, the four rating sources, and the staging-incubation steps.

panels.title: Worker — task outcomes (per min)
panels.description: Every handler return, by outcome: done (real work), skipped (data already fresh — the queue handed out work that was no longer needed), rescheduled (transient retry/backoff), failed (handler threw), no_handler (claimed mid-deploy on a node without the handler). A spike in skipped/rescheduled is wasted churn; failed is the error signal.

panels.title: Worker — fully-worked task duration (done, all types)
panels.description: Handler wall-clock of done tasks, percentiles over the bucketed histogram (kinowo_worker_task_duration_seconds). Skipped/failed runs are excluded. A rising p95/p99 means the upstreams (cinema sites, TMDB/IMDb, Filmweb/RT/Metacritic) or the scrape are getting slower.

panels.title: Worker — queue depth
panels.description: Live size of the durable task queue: waiting (claimable) + worked_on (leased by a worker right now), from worker /metrics. worked_on tracks pool utilization against pool size (the worker runs poolSize workers, one task each). A climbing 'waiting' is a backlog the pool isn't draining — the thing to correlate with the worker CPU-credit/steal panels above.

panels.title: Worker — waiting tasks by type
panels.description: Current backlog broken down by task type (sampled from the bounded active snapshot). Shows WHICH work is piling up — e.g. a wave of staging-incubation steps, or rating refreshes backing up behind a slow source.

panels.title: Worker — oldest waiting task age by type (s)
panels.description: Head-of-line latency: how long the oldest still-waiting task of each type has sat in the queue. A steadily climbing line is starvation — that type's work isn't being picked up (no handler, perpetual backoff, or the pool saturated by another type).

panels.title: Worker — fully-worked task duration p95 by type (s)
panels.description: Per-type tail latency of done (not skipped) tasks: the 95th-percentile handler wall-clock for each task type, over the bucketed histogram (kinowo_worker_task_duration_seconds). Splits the overall percentiles panel above by task_type so a slowdown localises to one upstream — e.g. ScrapeCinema (a cinema site), the *Rating types (Filmweb/RT/Metacritic/IMDb), or TMDB/IMDb resolution. Change 0.95 → 0.50 for the median.

panels.title: Worker — fully-worked task duration p50 (median) by type (s)
panels.description: Per-type typical latency of done (not skipped) tasks: the 50th-percentile (median) handler wall-clock for each task type, over the bucketed histogram (kinowo_worker_task_duration_seconds). The median companion to the p95-by-type panel above — p50 reads the everyday cost of each work kind, p95 the tail.

panels.title: Worker — staging incubation: movies by step (total = all in staging)
panels.description: Films currently incubating in pending_movies, stacked by the step each needs NEXT on its way into the live `movies` collection: detail (a cinema's synopsis/poster still owed) → resolve_tmdb (no TMDB match yet) → resolve_imdb (TMDB hit, IMDb cross-ref pending) → fold (concluded, about to graduate). Distinct films (a film's multiple cinema rows count once). The stacked total is the total movie count in staging; a step that keeps growing is where incubation is stalling (correlate with the staging task queue panels + StagingStuckAlerter).

panels.title: Worker — movie-row merges by reason & splits (per min)
panels.description: Rate of movie rows folded into another row per reason (kinowo_worker_merges_total — one increment per VICTIM absorbed; a cluster of N counts N−1). canonicalize=periodic same-film settle/rehydrate fold (cross-language same-tmdbId rows, or a ±year/spelling drift of one title); resolved-settle=a yearless stray absorbed when TMDB concludes a year; tmdb-identity=the runtime put-time same-tmdbId gate; normalize-rebuild=a title-rule change re-keys rows that now share a key and unions them. Each fold abandons the victim's title|year key, orphaning its DETAIL freshness — so a canonicalize spike here precedes the EnrichDetails → ResolveTmdb re-enrichment cascade that throttled the worker on 2026-06-19. The splits series (kinowo_worker_splits_total) is the inverse — rows a title-rule change un-merged, counted as new rows spawned (a 1→N split = N−1), each born fresh and re-resolving; normalize-rebuild merges are the re-merge half of a rebuild, splits the un-merge half.

panels.title: kinowo — films served per city (all future showings)
panels.description: Distinct films the web is currently serving on each /:city/ page (every film with a future showing), sampled once a minute from the real serving path (toSchedules) and exposed on web /metrics as kinowo_web_movies_served{scope="all"}. A city dropping to 0 here is the read-model-outage signal (a malformed web_movies row empties the whole corpus). The MoviesServedSwing + MoviesServedCityEmpty alerts watch this series.

panels.title: kinowo — films served per city (showing tomorrow)
panels.description: Distinct films each /:city/ page shows for tomorrow (≥1 showtime on the next local calendar day, Europe/Warsaw), from kinowo_web_movies_served{scope="tomorrow"}. Same per-minute sample as the all-future panel; the next-day view that fills the gap as today's showings pass.

panels.title: kinowo — films the movies collection would serve per city (all future)
panels.description: The SOURCE-collection mirror of the web panel above. Worker-side count of distinct films the live `movies` collection would serve on each /:city/ page (every film with a future showing), sampled off-band every 5 min by WorkerSourceFilmsMetrics through the REAL projection path and gated on the same readyToProject rule the projector writes by — exposed on worker /metrics as kinowo_worker_movies_served{scope="all"}. Compare against kinowo_web_movies_served{scope="all"}: in steady state the two match per city, so a city where worker > web means the read model (web_movies) has drifted behind the corpus — a stuck projection or a malformed/pruned web_movies row.

panels.title: kinowo — films the movies collection would serve per city (showing tomorrow)
panels.description: Source-collection mirror of the web tomorrow panel above, from kinowo_worker_movies_served{scope="tomorrow"} (worker-side, projected from `movies`, sampled every 5 min). Compare against kinowo_web_movies_served{scope="tomorrow"}; a per-city gap is the same read-model-drift signal as the all-future pair.

panels.title: Worker — movies corpus coverage (records, ids, ratings)
panels.description: Census of the live `movies` collection, sampled off-band every 5 min by WorkerCorpusMetrics and exposed as kinowo_worker_corpus_movies{subset}. total = distinct movie records; with_any_rating = at least one of imdb/fw/rt/mc; with_tmdb_id / with_imdb_id = resolution coverage; imdb_rating/rt_rating/mc_rating/fw_rating = per-source rating coverage. The gap between total and with_*_id is the resolution backlog; the gap between with_tmdb_id and each rating series is each site's enrichment backlog.

panels.title: Worker — movies change-stream events (per min, by op)
panels.description: Change events the shared cursor consumes from the movies collection, by operation. rate() is the change-stream volume the cache + read-model projector reproject from.

panels.title: Worker — change-stream UPDATE field kinds (per min)
panels.description: For UPDATE events, which field kind changed: source_data = a cinema slot (scrape write), rating, identity = tmdb/imdb id + resolution, updated_at_only = a no-op touching only updatedAt (redundant-write canary — should stay ~0).

panels.title: kinowo — total upcoming showtimes served (all cities)
panels.description: The general total of individual upcoming showtimes (single dated slots) the source `movies` collection would serve across every city — sum(kinowo_worker_showtimes), sampled off-band every 5 min by WorkerShowtimesMetrics through the REAL projection path, gated on the same readyToProject rule the projector writes by, counting only slots still in the future in each city's own zone. The slot-volume complement to the films-served panels above: films can hold steady while showtime volume swings (a chain adds evening slots, a venue drops a screen). A cliff here with the film count flat means slots were lost without a whole film disappearing.

panels.title: kinowo — upcoming showtimes served per city
panels.description: Per-city breakdown of the panel to the left: individual upcoming showtimes (single dated slots) the source `movies` collection would serve on each /:city/ page, from kinowo_worker_showtimes{city} (worker-side, projected from `movies`, sampled every 5 min, upcoming-only, readyToProject-gated). Every slot belongs to exactly one city, so these series sum to the total panel. A single city's showtime volume cratering while its film count holds is a venue that lost screens/sessions.

panels.title: Worker — JVM heap & host free memory (vs -Xmx)
panels.description: kinowo-worker heap detail + how much RAM the machine has left, scaled to the heap band (RSS and the 1GB machine-total line are omitted so this reads at ~0-320MB). JVM heap / old-gen / non-heap are the worker's own exports (app-metrics-live), scoped to $country by an `and on(app)` data join; host FREE is a Fly host metric (fly-prometheus, ~15-25min lag), scoped to $country through the hidden $worker_host_app variable — fly_instance_* carries no country label and sits on a different datasource, so a data join cannot reach it and the derived app list is the only way to narrow it. Old gen (tenured) rides ~205MB of the ~309MB max heap (-Xmx320m), i.e. ~90% full at rest — so a whole-corpus read-model reprojection's transient allocation tips it into a SerialGC full-GC thrash (VM Thread ~0.6 core) that drains CPU credit to the floor (seen 2026-07-03 20:40). 'Host free' is the machine's remaining RAM: hovering ~200MB, it leaves little room to raise -Xmx on this box. Its twin to the right is the same view of the WEB app.

panels.title: Web — JVM heap & host free memory (vs -Xmx)
panels.description: The WEB app's heap, read exactly like the worker panel to its left. The web JVM's own jvm_* exports carry no country label (one process per country, so the app label already implies it) — they are scoped to $country by intersecting with kinowo_web_movies_served{country}, the same `and on(app)` data join the worker panel uses against kinowo_worker_throttled. Host FREE is narrowed to $country through the hidden $web_host_app variable, the mirror of the worker panel's $worker_host_app — fly_instance_* has no country label, so that derived app list is what scopes it. Different tuning from the worker, so read the shape differently: web runs G1 at -Xmx384m (fly.toml JAVA_OPTS), so 'Old gen (G1)' sawtooths as concurrent cycles reclaim rather than sitting pinned ~90% full, and a rising floor across cycles — not a single spike — is the leak signal.

panels.title: kinowo-worker CPU vs credit-earn breakeven (centi-cores) — sustained ABOVE the line drains credit
panels.description: Worker CPU in centi-cores (100 = 1 core). 'java (fresh)' is the JVM's own process CPU from the app-metrics sidecar (~15s fresh, leading); 'host busy (authoritative)' is sum(rate(fly_instance_cpu{mode!=idle|steal})) — what Fly actually bills credit against (~15-25min lagged). The dashed breakeven line (~13cc) is the shared-cpu-2x credit EARN rate (empirical): while CPU sits ABOVE it the credit bucket drains, below it refills. Dead-flat CPU pinned above the line with no dips = a spin (the JDK-NIO2 epoll wedge) OR a structural overload — a reboot re-grant just re-drains; a bursty CPU that dips well under the line rebuilds credit. Pair with 'CPU credit balance'.

panels.title: kinowo-worker CPU drivers — projection vs JIT vs other (stacked = total CPU)
panels.description: STACKED-AREA breakdown of the worker's process CPU (centi-cores). The three areas sum to total CPU, so at a glance you see how much of the ~30cc daytime load is READ-MODEL PROJECTION vs JIT vs everything else. projection (red) = rate(kinowo_worker_readmodel_project_duration_seconds_sum)*100 — the pure ReadModelProjection.projectAll CPU (the 2026-07 credit-floor driver: synopsisByCity re-projecting festival/wide-release films on every screenings change, cut hard by synopsis memoization). JIT (purple) = rate(jvm_compilation_time_seconds_total)*100 — boot warm-up (transient). other (grey) = process CPU − projection − JIT — scrape/enrich parsing + Mongo I/O + GC. The dashed 'process CPU total' overlay is a NON-stacked sanity line: the three areas should sum to it. The ~13cc line is the CPU-credit earn/burn breakeven. NOTE the load is DAY-shaped: ~30cc daytime, ~5cc overnight (schedules static → no screenings changes → no projection). Per-second trigger/output rates are in the companion panel below (different unit — can't stack with cc).

panels.title: kinowo-worker projection triggers & output (per-sec)
panels.description: The per-second rates that DRIVE and RESULT FROM projection — split out of the CPU panel above because they're a different unit (events/s, not centi-cores) and can't stack with cc. change-events/s is the projection TRIGGER (a movies/screenings write); projection-calls/s is how many rows projectAll ran on; readmodel-writes/s is the OUTPUT (post-dedup, near-0 even when projection is hot — which is exactly why raw writes under-report projection CPU and the duration metric above was needed).

panels.title: Read-model projection efficiency
panels.description: How hard the read-model projection works. Over-triggering shows as high projection calls/s (the reproject/enrich churn re-projecting rows); an expensive-film tail shows as high p99 ms (one row whose resolve/synopsis work is costly). p50/p95/p99 are per-projection wall-clock in ms (LEFT axis); projections/s is the trigger rate (RIGHT axis). After the metadata-reuse cache, showtime-only churn keeps the percentiles low even when calls/s is high.

panels.title: projection duration heatmap — bright band high = expensive-film spikes
panels.description: Distribution of per-projection wall-clock over time, from the readmodel_project_duration_seconds histogram buckets. A bright band that jumps to a higher latency row = a burst of expensive-film projections (the resolve/synopsis tail); a band pinned low = cheap projections (incl. the metadata-reuse fast path recomputing only the screenings half).
